clean_story:
Liquid steel threads. Nature’s bulletproof fabric.

The name spider silk comes from its source—spiders spin it—but "silk" undersells it. A strand thinner than human hair stops a bee in mid-flight like a nylon rope halting a truck.

Spiders don’t weave; they pull. Inside their abdomens, protein soup hardens into fibers as they draw it through spinnerets—tiny nozzles acting like a 3D printer’s hotend. Orb-weavers adjust tension mid-spin, tightening strands for impact or loosening them for stretch, the way a guitarist tunes strings for different notes. Darwin’s bark spider spins silk 10 times tougher than Kevlar, yet its web sags under dew, not rain—a calculated trade-off for energy.

A golden orb-weaver’s dragline (its lifeline) can absorb 40% of its length in stretch before snapping. Compare that to a rubber band’s 20%. Military labs now breed transgenic silkworms with spider genes to mass-produce body armor lighter than cotton.

Three related subjects are bioengineered materials, predator-prey arms races, and self-assembling structures.
